title: The Post Office
text_zh: 我家附近的邮局是一栋绿色的老房子，已经有三十多年了。里面的装修很简单，地上铺着白色的瓷砖，墙上挂着价格表和寄件须知。柜台后面坐着两个工作人员，一个负责寄信和包裹，另一个负责汇款和其他业务。现在来邮局的人比以前少多了。以前过年的时候这里排队排到门外面，大家都来寄年货和明信片。现在大部分人都用快递和电子转账了，邮局变得安静了。但是有一些事情还是只有邮局能做：寄挂号信、办理邮政储蓄、买邮票。上周我来寄一封挂号信，工作人员很仔细地帮我称了重量，贴了邮票，在信封上盖了邮戳。那个邮戳的声音——「咔嗒」一下——让我突然觉得很踏实。在一个什么都变快的世界里，邮局还在用它自己的速度做事。也许不是所有事情都需要快，有些事情值得慢慢来。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn de yóujú shì yí dòng lǜsè de lǎo fángzi, yǐjīng yǒu sānshí duō nián le. Lǐmiàn de zhuāngxiū hěn jiǎndān, dì shàng pū zhe báisè de cízhuān, qiáng shàng guà zhe jiàgé biǎo hé jì jiàn xūzhī. Guìtái hòumiàn zuò zhe liǎng gè gōngzuò rényuán, yí gè fùzé jì xìn hé bāoguǒ, lìng yí gè fùzé huìkuǎn hé qítā yèwù. Xiànzài lái yóujú de rén bǐ yǐqián shǎo duō le. Yǐqián guò nián de shíhou zhèlǐ páiduì pái dào mén wàimiàn, dàjiā dōu lái jì niánhuò hé míngxìnpiàn. Xiànzài dà bùfen rén dōu yòng kuàidì hé diànzǐ zhuǎnzhàng le, yóujú biàn de ānjìng le. Dànshì yǒu yìxiē shìqíng háishi zhǐ yǒu yóujú néng zuò: jì guàhào xìn, bànlǐ yóuzhèng chǔxù, mǎi yóupiào. Shàng zhōu wǒ lái jì yì fēng guàhào xìn, gōngzuò rényuán hěn zǐxì de bāng wǒ chēng le zhòngliàng, tiē le yóupiào, zài xìnfēng shàng gài le yóuchuō. Nàge yóuchuō de shēngyīn —— 「kādā」yíxià —— ràng wǒ tūrán juéde hěn tāshi. Zài yí gè shénme dōu biàn kuài de shìjiè lǐ, yóujú hái zài yòng tā zìjǐ de sùdù zuò shì. Yěxǔ bú shì suǒyǒu shìqíng dōu xūyào kuài, yǒuxiē shìqíng zhíde mànmàn lái.
text_en: The post office near my home is an old green building, over thirty years old. The interior is simple — white floor tiles, a price list and mailing guidelines on the wall. Behind the counter sit two workers, one handling letters and packages, the other money transfers and other services. Far fewer people come to the post office now. During New Year's it used to have lines stretching outside, everyone mailing New Year goods and postcards. Now most people use express delivery and electronic transfers, so the post office has become quiet. But some things only the post office can do: sending registered mail, postal savings, buying stamps. Last week I came to send a registered letter. The worker carefully weighed it, affixed stamps, and stamped the postmark on the envelope. That postmark sound — 'click' — suddenly made me feel grounded. In a world where everything gets faster, the post office still works at its own pace. Maybe not everything needs to be fast; some things are worth taking slowly.

questions.q_zh: 现在邮局为什么比以前安静了？
questions.q_en: Why is the post office quieter now than before?

options.text: 大部分人用快递和电子转账了
options.pinyin: dà bùfen rén yòng kuàidì hé diànzǐ zhuǎnzhàng le
options.text_en: Most people use express delivery and electronic transfers

questions.q_zh: 邮戳的声音让作者有什么感觉？
questions.q_en: How did the postmark sound make the author feel?

title: The Teacher's Desk
text_zh: 我上高中的时候，班主任李老师的办公桌总是堆满了东西。试卷、作业本、参考书一摞一摞地堆着，但是她总能在几秒钟之内找到需要的东西。她的桌子虽然看起来很乱，但其实有自己的系统。左边一摞是还没批改的作业，右边是已经批改好的。中间放着一个红色的笔筒，里面插满了红色的笔——她说红笔是老师最重要的工具。桌子角上有一个小台灯和一杯茶，茶早就凉了，因为她每次倒完茶就忘了喝。每天放学以后，总有学生来找她问问题。她会把椅子拉出来让学生坐，自己站着讲。讲完以后她会问「听懂了吗？」如果学生说听懂了，她会再出一道类似的题让他做，确认真的懂了才放人走。二十年过去了，我还记得那张堆满东西的桌子，还记得那杯永远凉着的茶。有些系统不在电脑里，在一个人的习惯里。
text_pinyin: Wǒ shàng gāozhōng de shíhou, bānzhǔrèn Lǐ lǎoshī de bàngōngzhuō zǒng shì duī mǎn le dōngxi. Shìjuàn, zuòyè běn, cānkǎo shū yì luò yì luò de duī zhe, dànshì tā zǒng néng zài jǐ miǎozhōng zhī nèi zhǎo dào xūyào de dōngxi. Tā de zhuōzi suīrán kàn qǐlái hěn luàn, dàn qíshí yǒu zìjǐ de xìtǒng. Zuǒbiān yì luò shì hái méi pīgǎi de zuòyè, yòubiān shì yǐjīng pīgǎi hǎo de. Zhōngjiān fàng zhe yí gè hóngsè de bǐtǒng, lǐmiàn chā mǎn le hóngsè de bǐ — tā shuō hóng bǐ shì lǎoshī zuì zhòngyào de gōngjù. Zhuōzi jiǎo shàng yǒu yí gè xiǎo táidēng hé yì bēi chá, chá zǎo jiù liáng le, yīnwèi tā měi cì dào wán chá jiù wàng le hē. Měi tiān fàngxué yǐhòu, zǒng yǒu xuéshēng lái zhǎo tā wèn wèntí. Tā huì bǎ yǐzi lā chūlái ràng xuéshēng zuò, zìjǐ zhàn zhe jiǎng. Jiǎng wán yǐhòu tā huì wèn「tīng dǒng le ma?」Rúguǒ xuéshēng shuō tīng dǒng le, tā huì zài chū yí dào lèisì de tí ràng tā zuò, quèrèn zhēn de dǒng le cái fàng rén zǒu. Èrshí nián guòqù le, wǒ hái jìde nà zhāng duī mǎn dōngxi de zhuōzi, hái jìde nà bēi yǒngyuǎn liáng zhe de chá. Yǒuxiē xìtǒng bú zài diànnǎo lǐ, zài yí gè rén de xíguàn lǐ.
text_en: When I was in high school, our homeroom teacher Ms. Li's desk was always piled with things. Test papers, homework books, reference books stacked in piles, yet she could always find what she needed within seconds. Though her desk looked messy, it actually had its own system. The left pile was ungraded homework, the right was already graded. In the middle sat a red pen holder full of red pens — she said red pens are a teacher's most important tool. On the desk corner was a small lamp and a cup of tea, the tea long gone cold because she always forgot to drink after pouring. After school every day, students would come to ask questions. She'd pull out her chair for the student to sit while she stood to explain. After explaining she'd ask 'do you understand?' If the student said yes, she'd give them a similar problem to solve, only letting them leave after confirming they truly understood. Twenty years have passed and I still remember that desk piled with things, still remember that forever-cold cup of tea. Some systems don't exist in computers — they exist in a person's habits.

questions.q_zh: 李老师桌子上的系统是什么？
questions.q_en: What was the system on Ms. Li's desk?

options.text: 左边没批改的，右边批改好的
options.pinyin: zuǒbiān méi pīgǎi de, yòubiān pīgǎi hǎo de
options.text_en: Left side ungraded, right side graded

options.pinyin: dōu yòng wénjiàn jiā zhuāng hǎo

questions.q_zh: 学生说听懂了以后李老师会怎么做？
questions.q_en: What would Ms. Li do after a student said they understood?

options.text: 再出一道类似的题确认真的懂了
options.pinyin: zài chū yí dào lèisì de tí quèrèn zhēn de dǒng le
options.text_en: Give a similar problem to confirm understanding

options.pinyin: jìxù jiǎng xià yí gè nèiróng

title: The Recycling Routine
text_zh: 自从实行垃圾分类以后，扔垃圾变成了一件需要思考的事。以前是一个袋子装所有的垃圾，现在要分成四种：可回收、有害垃圾、湿垃圾和干垃圾。刚开始的时候大家都很不习惯，经常分错。骨头是湿垃圾还是干垃圾？用过的纸巾是可回收还是干垃圾？这些问题每天都有人在讨论。小区里有一个志愿者大妈，每天早上七点和晚上七点守在垃圾桶旁边，帮大家检查分类。她戴着红色的袖章，手里拿着一个夹子，看到分错的会用夹子帮你挑出来，然后微笑着告诉你应该放在哪个桶里。半年以后，大部分人已经养成了习惯。我现在做饭的时候就会自动把菜叶放一个袋子，包装纸放另一个袋子。这个习惯改变了我对垃圾的看法——它不是一堆无用的东西，而是可以被分类、被回收、被重新利用的资源。一个好的系统可以慢慢改变人的行为。
text_pinyin: Zìcóng shíxíng lājī fēnlèi yǐhòu, rēng lājī biàn chéng le yí jiàn xūyào sīkǎo de shì. Yǐqián shì yí gè dàizi zhuāng suǒyǒu de lājī, xiànzài yào fēn chéng sì zhǒng: kě huíshōu, yǒuhài lājī, shī lājī hé gān lājī. Gāng kāishǐ de shíhou dàjiā dōu hěn bù xíguàn, jīngcháng fēn cuò. Gǔtou shì shī lājī háishi gān lājī? Yòng guò de zhǐjīn shì kě huíshōu háishi gān lājī? Zhèxiē wèntí měi tiān dōu yǒu rén zài tǎolùn. Xiǎoqū lǐ yǒu yí gè zhìyuànzhě dàmā, měi tiān zǎoshang qī diǎn hé wǎnshang qī diǎn shǒu zài lājī tǒng pángbiān, bāng dàjiā jiǎnchá fēnlèi. Tā dài zhe hóngsè de xiùzhāng, shǒu lǐ ná zhe yí gè jiāzi, kàn dào fēn cuò de huì yòng jiāzi bāng nǐ tiāo chūlái, ránhòu wēixiào zhe gàosu nǐ yīnggāi fàng zài nǎge tǒng lǐ. Bàn nián yǐhòu, dà bùfen rén yǐjīng yǎng chéng le xíguàn. Wǒ xiànzài zuòfàn de shíhou jiù huì zìdòng bǎ cài yè fàng yí gè dàizi, bāozhuāng zhǐ fàng lìng yí gè dàizi. Zhège xíguàn gǎibiàn le wǒ duì lājī de kànfǎ — tā bú shì yì duī wúyòng de dōngxi, ér shì kěyǐ bèi fēnlèi, bèi huíshōu, bèi chóngxīn lìyòng de zīyuán. Yí gè hǎo de xìtǒng kěyǐ mànmàn gǎibiàn rén de xíngwéi.
text_en: Since waste sorting was implemented, throwing out trash became something requiring thought. Before, one bag held everything; now it's four categories: recyclable, hazardous, wet waste, and dry waste. At first nobody was used to it, constantly sorting wrong. Are bones wet waste or dry waste? Are used tissues recyclable or dry waste? These questions were discussed daily. A volunteer auntie in our compound stands by the bins at 7 AM and 7 PM every day, helping everyone check their sorting. She wears a red armband and holds tongs — when she spots something misclassified, she picks it out with the tongs and smilingly tells you which bin it belongs in. Six months later, most people had formed the habit. Now while cooking I automatically put vegetable leaves in one bag and packaging paper in another. This habit changed how I see trash — it's not a pile of useless stuff, but resources that can be sorted, recycled, and reused. A good system can gradually change people's behavior.

questions.q_en: How many categories is waste sorted into?

questions.q_zh: 志愿者大妈看到分错的垃圾会怎么做？
questions.q_en: What does the volunteer auntie do when she sees misclassified trash?

options.text: 用夹子帮忙挑出来，微笑着告诉你放哪里
options.pinyin: yòng jiāzi bāngmáng tiāo chūlái, wēixiào zhe gàosu nǐ fàng nǎlǐ
options.text_en: Picks it out with tongs and smilingly tells you where to put it

title: The Canteen Auntie
text_zh: 公司食堂有一位阿姨，大家都叫她张姐。她负责午餐的最后一个窗口——打汤的。每天中午她站在一大锅汤后面，手里拿着一个大勺子，面带微笑等着大家来盛汤。张姐有一个特别的习惯：她会记住每个人喜欢喝什么汤。看到不喜欢喝鸡蛋汤的人来了，她会主动说「今天有你喜欢的排骨汤，在旁边那个锅里」。有些新来的同事不知道这个，第一次来盛汤的时候张姐会问「你喜欢喝什么汤？辣的还是不辣的？」然后在脑子里记下来。有人问她怎么记得住那么多人的口味，她说「天天见，自然就记住了」。食堂的菜有时候好吃有时候一般，但张姐打的汤永远是热的，碗永远是满的。她不只是一个打汤的人，她是食堂这个系统里最温暖的部分。每个看起来冰冷的系统里，都需要一个像张姐这样的人。
text_pinyin: Gōngsī shítáng yǒu yí wèi āyí, dàjiā dōu jiào tā Zhāng jiě. Tā fùzé wǔcān de zuìhòu yí gè chuāngkǒu — dǎ tāng de. Měi tiān zhōngwǔ tā zhàn zài yí dà guō tāng hòumiàn, shǒu lǐ ná zhe yí gè dà sháozi, miàn dài wēixiào děng zhe dàjiā lái chéng tāng. Zhāng jiě yǒu yí gè tèbié de xíguàn: tā huì jì zhù měi gè rén xǐhuan hē shénme tāng. Kàn dào bù xǐhuan hē jīdàn tāng de rén lái le, tā huì zhǔdòng shuō「jīntiān yǒu nǐ xǐhuan de páigǔ tāng, zài pángbiān nàge guō lǐ」. Yǒuxiē xīn lái de tóngshì bù zhīdào zhège, dì yī cì lái chéng tāng de shíhou Zhāng jiě huì wèn「nǐ xǐhuan hē shénme tāng? Là de háishi bú là de?」Ránhòu zài nǎozi lǐ jì xiàlái. Yǒu rén wèn tā zěnme jì de zhù nàme duō rén de kǒuwèi, tā shuō「tiāntiān jiàn, zìrán jiù jì zhù le」. Shítáng de cài yǒu shíhou hǎochī yǒu shíhou yìbān, dàn Zhāng jiě dǎ de tāng yǒngyuǎn shì rè de, wǎn yǒngyuǎn shì mǎn de. Tā bù zhǐ shì yí gè dǎ tāng de rén, tā shì shítáng zhège xìtǒng lǐ zuì wēnnuǎn de bùfen. Měi gè kàn qǐlái bīnglěng de xìtǒng lǐ, dōu xūyào yí gè xiàng Zhāng jiě zhèyàng de rén.
text_en: Our company cafeteria has an auntie everyone calls Sister Zhang. She handles the last counter at lunch — serving soup. Every noon she stands behind a big pot of soup, holding a large ladle, smiling as she waits for everyone. Sister Zhang has a special habit: she remembers what soup each person likes. When she sees someone who doesn't like egg soup, she'll say 'today there's your favorite pork rib soup, in the pot next to this one.' New colleagues don't know this — the first time they come for soup, Sister Zhang asks 'What soup do you like? Spicy or not?' then memorizes it. Someone asked how she remembers so many people's preferences. She said 'I see everyone every day, naturally I remember.' The cafeteria food is sometimes good, sometimes average, but Sister Zhang's soup is always hot and the bowls always full. She's not just someone who serves soup — she's the warmest part of the cafeteria system. Every seemingly cold system needs someone like Sister Zhang.

questions.q_zh: 张姐有什么特别的习惯？
questions.q_en: What special habit does Sister Zhang have?

options.pinyin: měi tiān huàn yì zhǒng tāng

options.text: 记住每个人喜欢喝什么汤
options.pinyin: jì zhù měi gè rén xǐhuan hē shénme tāng
options.text_en: Remembers what soup each person likes

options.pinyin: duō gěi měi gè rén yì wǎn
zìjǐ xiān cháng tāng de wèidào

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得张姐是食堂系统里的什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think Sister Zhang is in the cafeteria system?

title: The Shared Bicycle
text_zh: 共享单车改变了城市里「最后一公里」的问题。从地铁站出来以后，走路回家要二十分钟，但骑共享单车只要五分钟。用手机扫一下车上的二维码，听到「咔嗒」一声锁打开了，就可以骑了。到了目的地，把车停在指定的区域，关上锁，手机上自动扣费。整个过程不需要和任何人说话。但是这个系统也有不完美的地方。有时候车子坏了没有人修，刹车不好用或者座椅太高调不下来。有时候找不到可以停车的地方，只好把车停在很远的地方再走一段。还有些人把共享单车乱停在人行道上，影响了行人走路。我的邻居老赵每天早上都会把小区门口乱停的共享单车整理好。没有人要求他这样做，也没有人给他钱。他说「公共的东西就应该大家一起维护」。也许这就是共享的真正意思——不只是共享使用权，也共享责任。
text_pinyin: Gòngxiǎng dānchē gǎibiàn le chéngshì lǐ「zuìhòu yì gōnglǐ」de wèntí. Cóng dìtiě zhàn chūlái yǐhòu, zǒulù huí jiā yào èrshí fēnzhōng, dàn qí gòngxiǎng dānchē zhǐ yào wǔ fēnzhōng. Yòng shǒujī sǎo yíxià chē shàng de èrwéimǎ, tīng dào「kādā」yì shēng suǒ dǎkāi le, jiù kěyǐ qí le. Dào le mùdìdì, bǎ chē tíng zài zhǐdìng de qūyù, guān shàng suǒ, shǒujī shàng zìdòng kòu fèi. Zhěnggè guòchéng bù xūyào hé rènhé rén shuōhuà. Dànshì zhège xìtǒng yě yǒu bù wánměi de dìfāng. Yǒu shíhou chēzi huài le méiyǒu rén xiū, shāchē bù hǎo yòng huòzhě zuòyǐ tài gāo tiáo bù xiàlái. Yǒu shíhou zhǎo bú dào kěyǐ tíng chē de dìfāng, zhǐhǎo bǎ chē tíng zài hěn yuǎn de dìfāng zài zǒu yí duàn. Hái yǒu xiē rén bǎ gòngxiǎng dānchē luàn tíng zài rénxíngdào shàng, yǐngxiǎng le xíngrén zǒulù. Wǒ de línjū Lǎo Zhào měi tiān zǎoshang dōu huì bǎ xiǎoqū ménkǒu luàn tíng de gòngxiǎng dānchē zhěnglǐ hǎo. Méiyǒu rén yāoqiú tā zhèyàng zuò, yě méiyǒu rén gěi tā qián. Tā shuō「gōnggòng de dōngxi jiù yīnggāi dàjiā yìqǐ wéihù」. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì gòngxiǎng de zhēnzhèng yìsi — bù zhǐ shì gòngxiǎng shǐyòng quán, yě gòngxiǎng zérèn.
text_en: Shared bicycles solved the 'last mile' problem in cities. Walking home from the subway takes twenty minutes, but riding a shared bike takes only five. Scan the QR code on the bike with your phone, hear the lock 'click' open, and ride. At your destination, park in the designated area, close the lock, and fees are automatically deducted on your phone. The whole process requires no human interaction. But the system isn't perfect. Sometimes bikes are broken with nobody to fix them — bad brakes or seats stuck too high. Sometimes you can't find a parking spot and must park far away and walk the rest. Some people park shared bikes randomly on sidewalks, blocking pedestrians. My neighbor Old Zhao tidies up the messily parked shared bikes at our compound entrance every morning. Nobody asks him to, nobody pays him. He says 'public things should be maintained by everyone together.' Maybe that's the true meaning of sharing — not just sharing the right to use, but sharing the responsibility.

questions.q_zh: 共享单车解决了什么问题？
questions.q_en: What problem do shared bikes solve?
What does Old Zhao do every morning?

options.text_en: Rides a shared bike to work

options.pinyin: zhěnglǐ luàn tíng de gòngxiǎng dānchē
options.text_en: Tidies up messily parked shared bikes
Teaches others how to use them

title: The Apartment Building Rules
text_zh: 住在一栋楼里的人，虽然大多互不相识，但慢慢地形成了一些没有人明确规定过的规则。比如：晚上十点以后不要大声说话或者放音乐；在电梯里遇到邻居要点个头或者微笑一下；不要在楼道里堆放东西；如果你家的狗在公共区域拉了屎，你要自己清理干净。这些规则没有写在任何地方，但大家都知道。偶尔有人不遵守——比如有一段时间六楼的一家人每天晚上十一点还在唱卡拉OK——其他邻居就会不高兴。一开始大家只是忍着，后来有人贴了一张条子在电梯里，写着「请注意休息时间」。条子贴了三天以后，唱卡拉OK的声音消失了。没有人直接去敲门说，但信息传达到了。我觉得这就是一种民间的自我管理系统。它不靠法律，不靠罚款，靠的是大家对共同生活空间的尊重。虽然有时候不完美，但大部分时候它是有效的。
text_pinyin: Zhù zài yí dòng lóu lǐ de rén, suīrán dàduō hù bù xiāngshí, dàn mànmàn de xíngchéng le yìxiē méiyǒu rén míngquè guīdìng guò de guīzé. Bǐrú: wǎnshang shí diǎn yǐhòu bú yào dà shēng shuōhuà huòzhě fàng yīnyuè; zài diàntī lǐ yù dào línjū yào diǎn gè tóu huòzhě wēixiào yíxià; bú yào zài lóudào lǐ duī fàng dōngxi; rúguǒ nǐ jiā de gǒu zài gōnggòng qūyù lā le shǐ, nǐ yào zìjǐ qīnglǐ gānjìng. Zhèxiē guīzé méiyǒu xiě zài rènhé dìfāng, dàn dàjiā dōu zhīdào. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén bù zūnshǒu — bǐrú yǒu yí duàn shíjiān liù lóu de yì jiā rén měi tiān wǎnshang shíyī diǎn hái zài chàng kǎlā OK — qítā línjū jiù huì bù gāoxìng. Yì kāishǐ dàjiā zhǐ shì rěn zhe, hòulái yǒu rén tiē le yì zhāng tiáozi zài diàntī lǐ, xiě zhe「qǐng zhùyì xiūxi shíjiān」. Tiáozi tiē le sān tiān yǐhòu, chàng kǎlā OK de shēngyīn xiāoshī le. Méiyǒu rén zhíjiē qù qiāo mén shuō, dàn xìnxī chuándá dào le. Wǒ juéde zhè jiù shì yì zhǒng mínjiān de zìwǒ guǎnlǐ xìtǒng. Tā bú kào fǎlǜ, bú kào fákuǎn, kào de shì dàjiā duì gòngtóng shēnghuó kōngjiān de zūnzhòng. Suīrán yǒu shíhou bù wánměi, dàn dà bùfen shíhou tā shì yǒuxiào de.
text_en: People living in the same building, though mostly strangers, gradually form rules nobody has explicitly set. For example: no loud talking or music after 10 PM; nod or smile when meeting neighbors in the elevator; don't pile things in the hallways; if your dog poops in common areas, clean it up yourself. These rules aren't written anywhere, but everyone knows them. Occasionally someone breaks them — for a while, a family on the sixth floor sang karaoke every night at 11. Other neighbors were unhappy. At first everyone just endured it, then someone posted a note in the elevator: 'Please be mindful of rest hours.' Three days after the note, the karaoke noise disappeared. Nobody went to knock on the door directly, but the message got through. I think this is a kind of grassroots self-management system. It doesn't rely on laws or fines, but on everyone's respect for shared living space. Though sometimes imperfect, most of the time it works.

questions.q_zh: 六楼唱卡拉OK的问题怎么解决的？
questions.q_en: How was the sixth-floor karaoke problem solved?

options.text: 有人在电梯里贴了一张条子
options.pinyin: yǒu rén zài diàntī lǐ tiē le yì zhāng tiáozi
options.text_en: Someone posted a note in the elevator

questions.q_zh: 这种自我管理系统靠的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does this self-management system rely on?

options.text: 大家对共同生活空间的尊重
options.pinyin: dàjiā duì gòngtóng shēnghuó kōngjiān de zūnzhòng
options.text_en: Everyone's respect for shared living space

title: The Company Meeting
text_zh: 我们公司每周一上午九点有一个例会。说是例会，其实大家都知道它通常比需要的时间长。会议室有一张长桌子，能坐十二个人。老板坐在最里面的位置，面对着一块白板。每个部门的负责人轮流汇报上周的工作和这周的计划。汇报完以后老板会提问，有时候一个问题能讨论二十分钟。我发现了一个有趣的现象：坐得离老板越近的人说得越多，坐得越远的人越安静。我通常坐在最远的位置，带着笔记本假装在记录，其实在想中午吃什么。但偶尔老板会突然点名问我的意见，这时候就需要马上把注意力拉回来。会议结束以后大家走出会议室，表情各不相同。有人觉得会开得太长了，有人觉得终于把问题说清楚了。我觉得会议最好的部分其实是散会以后——大家在走廊里三三两两地聊几句，那些在会上没说的话反而在这个时候说出来了。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen gōngsī měi zhōu yī zǎoshang jiǔ diǎn yǒu yí gè lìhuì. Shuō shì lìhuì, qíshí dàjiā dōu zhīdào tā tōngcháng bǐ xūyào de shíjiān cháng. Huìyì shì yǒu yì zhāng cháng zhuōzi, néng zuò shí'èr gè rén. Lǎobǎn zuò zài zuì lǐmiàn de wèizhì, miàn duì zhe yí kuài báibǎn. Měi gè bùmén de fùzé rén lúnliú huìbào shàng zhōu de gōngzuò hé zhè zhōu de jìhuà. Huìbào wán yǐhòu lǎobǎn huì tíwèn, yǒu shíhou yí gè wèntí néng tǎolùn èrshí fēnzhōng. Wǒ fāxiàn le yí gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: zuò de lí lǎobǎn yuè jìn de rén shuō de yuè duō, zuò de yuè yuǎn de rén yuè ānjìng. Wǒ tōngcháng zuò zài zuì yuǎn de wèizhì, dài zhe bǐjìběn jiǎzhuāng zài jìlù, qíshí zài xiǎng zhōngwǔ chī shénme. Dàn ǒu'ěr lǎobǎn huì tūrán diǎnmíng wèn wǒ de yìjiàn, zhè shíhou jiù xūyào mǎshàng bǎ zhùyìlì lā huílái. Huìyì jiéshù yǐhòu dàjiā zǒu chū huìyì shì, biǎoqíng gè bù xiāngtóng. Yǒu rén juéde huì kāi de tài cháng le, yǒu rén juéde zhōngyú bǎ wèntí shuō qīngchu le. Wǒ juéde huìyì zuì hǎo de bùfen qíshí shì sàn huì yǐhòu — dàjiā zài zǒuláng lǐ sān sān liǎng liǎng de liáo jǐ jù, nàxiē zài huì shàng méi shuō de huà fǎn'ér zài zhège shíhou shuō chūlái le.
text_en: Our company has a regular Monday meeting at 9 AM. Though called a regular meeting, everyone knows it typically runs longer than necessary. The meeting room has a long table seating twelve. The boss sits at the far end facing a whiteboard. Each department head takes turns reporting last week's work and this week's plans. After reports the boss asks questions — sometimes one question sparks twenty minutes of discussion. I've noticed an interesting phenomenon: people sitting closer to the boss speak more; those farther away stay quieter. I usually sit in the farthest seat, notebook open pretending to take notes, actually thinking about what to eat for lunch. But occasionally the boss suddenly calls on me for my opinion, and I need to snap my attention back immediately. After the meeting everyone files out with varying expressions. Some feel the meeting ran too long; others feel issues were finally clarified. I think the best part of the meeting is actually after it ends — people cluster in twos and threes in the hallway chatting, and what wasn't said in the meeting gets said then.

questions.q_zh: 作者发现了什么有趣的现象？
questions.q_en: What interesting phenomenon did the author notice?

options.pinyin: lǎobǎn zǒng shì shuō de zuì duō
options.text_en: The boss always talks the most

options.text: 坐得离老板越近说得越多
options.pinyin: zuò de lí lǎobǎn yuè jìn shuō de yuè duō
options.text_en: Those sitting closer to the boss speak more

options.text: 每个人说的时间都一样
options.pinyin: měi gè rén shuō de shíjiān dōu yíyàng
options.text_en: Everyone speaks for the same amount of time

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得会议最好的部分是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is the best part of the meeting?

options.text: 散会以后走廊里的交谈
options.pinyin: sàn huì yǐhòu zǒuláng lǐ de jiāotán

title: The Parking Lot Attendant
text_zh: 我们小区的地下停车场有一位管理员，姓周，大家叫他周叔。他每天早上六点就到了，晚上十点才下班。他的工作看起来很简单：确保每辆车停在自己的车位上，处理停车纠纷，指挥进出的车辆。但其实他做的远不止这些。周叔记得几乎每辆车的车主是谁，什么时候通常出门，什么时候回来。他能通过发动机的声音判断是哪辆车来了。有一次我忘记关车窗就走了，是周叔打电话提醒我的。还有一次下大雨，地面上的水流进了停车场，周叔一个人用扫帚把水往外扫，衣服全湿了。他说「车泡了水可不行，那是人家的财产」。周叔的月工资不高，但他把这份工作做得非常认真。他说「做什么工作不重要，重要的是做得让自己满意」。每次开车进停车场，看到周叔站在那里朝我挥挥手，我就觉得今天回家了。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū de dìxià tíngchē chǎng yǒu yí wèi guǎnlǐyuán, xìng Zhōu, dàjiā jiào tā Zhōu shū. Tā měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn jiù dào le, wǎnshang shí diǎn cái xiàbān. Tā de gōngzuò kàn qǐlái hěn jiǎndān: quèbǎo měi liàng chē tíng zài zìjǐ de chēwèi shàng, chǔlǐ tíngchē jiūfēn, zhǐhuī jìn chū de chēliàng. Dàn qíshí tā zuò de yuǎn bù zhǐ zhèxiē. Zhōu shū jìde jīhū měi liàng chē de chēzhǔ shì shéi, shénme shíhou tōngcháng chūmén, shénme shíhou huílái. Tā néng tōngguò fādòngjī de shēngyīn pànduàn shì nǎ liàng chē lái le. Yǒu yí cì wǒ wàngjì guān chē chuāng jiù zǒu le, shì Zhōu shū dǎ diànhuà tíxǐng wǒ de. Hái yǒu yí cì xià dà yǔ, dìmiàn shàng de shuǐ liú jìn le tíngchē chǎng, Zhōu shū yí gè rén yòng sǎozhou bǎ shuǐ wǎng wài sǎo, yīfu quán shī le. Tā shuō「chē pào le shuǐ kě bù xíng, nà shì rénjiā de cáichǎn」. Zhōu shū de yuè gōngzī bù gāo, dàn tā bǎ zhè fèn gōngzuò zuò de fēicháng rènzhēn. Tā shuō「zuò shénme gōngzuò bú zhòngyào, zhòngyào de shì zuò de ràng zìjǐ mǎnyì」. Měi cì kāi chē jìn tíngchē chǎng, kàn dào Zhōu shū zhàn zài nàlǐ cháo wǒ huī huī shǒu, wǒ jiù juéde jīntiān huí jiā le.
text_en: Our compound's underground parking lot has an attendant surnamed Zhou — everyone calls him Uncle Zhou. He arrives at 6 AM and doesn't leave until 10 PM. His job looks simple: make sure every car is in its spot, handle parking disputes, direct vehicles in and out. But he actually does far more. Uncle Zhou remembers nearly every car's owner, when they usually leave, when they return. He can tell which car is coming by its engine sound. Once I forgot to close my car window and left — Uncle Zhou called to remind me. Another time during heavy rain, water flowed into the parking lot from the ground. Uncle Zhou swept it out alone with a broom, clothes completely soaked. He said 'can't let the cars get flooded — that's people's property.' Uncle Zhou's monthly salary isn't high, but he does the job extremely conscientiously. He says 'what job you do doesn't matter; what matters is doing it to your own satisfaction.' Every time I drive into the parking lot and see Uncle Zhou standing there waving at me, I feel like I'm home.

questions.q_zh: 周叔有什么特别的能力？
questions.q_en: What special ability does Uncle Zhou have?

options.text: 能通过发动机声音判断是哪辆车
options.pinyin: néng tōngguò fādòngjī shēngyīn pànduàn shì nǎ liàng chē
options.text_en: Can tell which car by engine sound

options.pinyin: néng jì zhù suǒyǒu chēpái hàomǎ
néng yùcè shénme shíhou xià yǔ
options.text_en: Can predict when it will rain

questions.q_zh: 周叔觉得做工作最重要的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does Uncle Zhou think is most important about work?

options.text: 别人觉得你做得好不好
options.pinyin: biéren juéde nǐ zuò de hǎo bù hǎo
options.text_en: Whether others think you do well
Doing it to your own satisfaction
